qualifier: Aegon V had Bloodraven arrested for Aenys's murder after being crowned
evidence_snippet: After King Aegon V Targaryen was crowned king, he had Bloodraven arrested for the murder of Aenys, as Brynden had violated Aenys's safe conduct.

qualifier: executed by Bloodraven who violated his safe conduct at the Great Council
evidence_snippet: as soon as he entered the capital he was seized by the gold cloaks and dragged to the Red Keep, where he was executed. His head was presented to the lords of the Great Council.

reason: The gold cloaks seized Aenys — instrumental agents of Bloodraven's execution order, not a direct Aenys-City Watch relationship

qualifier: came to the Great Council to put forth his claim to the throne
evidence_snippet: Lord Brynden 'Bloodraven' Rivers, called a Great Council, Aenys put forth his claim to the throne by writing from exile. Bloodraven lured Aenys to King's Landing with false promises of safe conduct.

reason: Title of Brynden Rivers — Brynden's role as Hand, not a separate Aenys-Hand relationship beyond the Bloodraven relationship already captured

qualifier: put forth his claim to the Iron Throne at the Great Council of 233 AC
evidence_snippet: Aenys put forth his claim to the throne by writing from exile. Bloodraven lured Aenys to King's Landing with false promises of safe conduct.

reason: Maekar's death created the succession crisis — contextual narrative, no direct Aenys-Maekar relationship
Bloodraven was sentenced to the Night's Watch — Bloodraven's punishment, no Aenys-Night's Watch relationship
Location where Aenys was dragged and executed — setting of his death, not a sustained Aenys-Red Keep relationship
The Wall is where Bloodraven was sent instead of execution — Bloodraven's fate, no Aenys-Wall relationship
